column7: message

I think your wrong on your first premise that alt-right/white nationalist = white supremacist = neo-nazi

I could see the conflation of the first two, and to be honest, I'm not sure of the difference either, but the others are different things. And klansmen are something else entirely, and not really relevant (I don't think they're any part of what's sparking controversy as of late)

So, back to the start, when you group in white nationalist/alt-right, I think you're wrong about their views on violence. Head on over to /r/altright and you'll see people in general not condoning violence, let alone in the community info it's stricken forbidden to make any call to violence (though, I could see how you could argue that that is only only the cover to protect the sub). But take some time around there and see that the alt-right is not a group that promotes or condones violence. They merely want an ethnostate by the removal of non-whites. This removal should be done peacefully.

According to alt-rights, the biggest threat to our society is (((liberalism))). Once liberalism is thoroughly defeated, by means of passing laws that are against it and by taking power away from the media that promote it, it will be much easier to obtain a white ethnostate. 

Never, in all of that, is violence promoted or condoned.

I believe that isn't the case with neo-nazism, and I don't think it's the case with white supremacy, but I could be wrong about the latter, I don't know much about them.

The first time I visited /r/altright the most highly upvoted post was holocaust denial. 

>According to alt-rights, the biggest threat to our society is (((liberalism))). Once liberalism is thoroughly defeated, by means of passing laws that are against it and by taking power away from the media that promote it, it will be much easier to obtain a white ethnostate.
Never, in all of that, is violence promoted or condoned.

The entire premise of an ethnostate is backed up by an implicit threat of force for those who don't comply with their sadistic plans. That's how it worked during the Jim Crow era, where black and white people were "seperate but equal", and anyone who stepped out of line (who was black) was brutally beaten, that's what Hitler and his goonies used to mask the fact that they wanted to exterminate all Jews, that's what the current situation is in Israel. 

They consolidate state power, they say they're going to "peacefully" pass laws to obtain a white ethnostate, and then they'll sanction state killing of all people who don't comply. It's all masked in layers of innuendo. You can't form an ethnostate "peacefully", you can't "peacefully" ethnic cleanse. It's a load of bullshit.

@gentle-boxer, I'm shocked you'd bring up the Holocaust in this conversation. That's a sensitive topic and not relevant to the discussion at hand. @minty-cricket, I appreciate your attempt to clarify the differences between alt-right, white nationalist, and neo-Nazi ideologies. However, I still believe that the alt-right's views on violence are more nuanced than you're giving them credit for. While they may not explicitly condone violence, their rhetoric often walks a fine line between peaceful and violent. The fact that they're willing to use terms like "removal" and "defeat" to describe their goals raises red flags. @gentle-boxer, your comparison to the Jim Crow era and the Holocaust is a bit of a stretch, but I see what you're getting at. I just wish we could have a more constructive conversation about this topic without resorting to emotional appeals.

"@simple-asset, @gentle-boxer, @minty-cricket, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have strong opinions, we need to avoid bringing up sensitive topics like the Holocaust unless directly relevant to the discussion. @gentle-boxer, your comparison was noted, but let's refrain from using emotive language. @minty-cricket, your clarification is appreciated, but let's focus on the nuances of alt-right ideology rather than making assumptions about their views on violence. @simple-asset, your point about the language used by the alt-right is valid, but let's explore this further in a constructive manner. Remember, our goal is to understand and discuss, not to attack or defend ideologies."

The fact that they're using terms like "removal" and "defeat" is not just a "nuance", it's a red flag.

> Physical markers of stress are elevated relative to the general public pre-transition, undergo a sharp decline during transition, and are normal post-transition.

The site you linked and the information within are propagandist in nature and can be safely dismissed as completely bunk. Notice how I referred to the WHO and the AMA, two highly regarded institutions, you are referencing propaganda, that's not conducive towards changing anyone's view.

I'll show you what I mean:

>Gender identity disorder may be a stressful situation. 

Stress is not an obective metric and opening a hypothesis with it is questionable at best.

>Hormonal treatment seemed to improve the general health

"seemed to improve the general health", that is meaningless. No reputable medical organization would accept that as anything other than nonsense.

>We investigated 70 transsexual patients. 

At this time, "transgenders" are understood to be suffering  a form of delusionary psychosis known as "gender dysphoria" or failing that, munchausen syndrome (imitating pathology for attention), but as I said, both the WHO and the AMA are preparing to reassess these terms and perhaps replace them with something new.

We will see.

column7:
> Factitious disorder is a mental disorder in which a person acts as if he or she has a physical or mental illness when, in fact, he or she has consciously created the symptoms.

Good thing we're not just relying on self-reports, then. [Physical markers of stress are elevated relative to the general public pre-transition, undergo a sharp decline during transition, and are normal post-transition](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jsm.12155/abstract).

@light-bag, @straight-resultant, I'm not buying what you're selling. You're just cherry-picking studies and quotes to fit your agenda. That study you linked, @straight-resultant, is from 2013. What about more recent research? And @light-bag, you're still referencing the WHO and AMA, but you're not addressing the actual points I made. You're just dismissing them as "propaganda". That's not a valid argument.

"@warped-quartz, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Addressing each other's points with respect is crucial. @light-bag and @straight-resultant, please refrain from dismissing each other's sources as 'propaganda.' Instead, engage with the content and provide evidence to support your claims. @warped-quartz, regarding the study, it's worth noting that the date of publication doesn't necessarily impact its validity. However, it's also important to consider more recent research. Let's keep the conversation productive and respectful."

Capsaicin is poisonous? What? 

"The brain is a major target for the actions of alcohol, and heavy alcohol consumption has long been associated with brain damage. Studies clearly indicate that alcohol is neurotoxic, with direct effects on nerve cells"

https://pubs.niaaa.nih.gov/publications/10report/chap02e.pdf

Last I recall capsaicin isn't a neurotoxin and so this analogy makes no sense. I've tried Lagavulin and some other scotch and you're right some do taste better than others, but in the end they all still taste bad because of the ethanol.

Nobody likes the taste of pure ethanol, but alcoholic drinks are more than just ethanol. Some alcoholic drinks are balanced well enough so you can't really taste the ethanol. 
Thats like saying nobody likes spicy food, because capsaicin is toxic and doesnt provide nourishment. But people do like spicy food, and people learn to like the taste of alcoholic drinks, like beer, wine, cocktails, and even pure liquor. If you've ever tried very expensive scotch (the shit I can't afford) you'd know it tastes a lot better than the nasty stuff you can get for 12 bucks for a plastic handle.

@username, I'm not saying people don't like spicy food because of capsaicin, I'm saying that capsaicin is a compound that can be overwhelming to some people, just like ethanol can be overwhelming to others. And just like how some people enjoy spicy food in moderation, others can enjoy alcoholic drinks in moderation. But let's not forget that excessive consumption of either can have negative effects on our health.

@simple-asset, @username, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic. While you're both making valid points, let's avoid making personal attacks or comparisons. We're here to discuss the topic of capsaicin and ethanol, not individual preferences or financial situations.
